text: Good afternoon. My name is Sarah and I will be your conference operator today.
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: At this time, I would like to welcome everyone to NVIDIA's first quarter earnings call.
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: All lines have been placed on you to prevent any background noise.
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: After the speaker's remarks, there will be a question and answer session.
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: If you would like to ask a question during this time,
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: simply press star followed by the number one on your telephone keypad.
speaker: TOSHIYA HARI - VP, Investor Relations & Strategic Finance

text: If you would like to withdraw your question, press star one again. Thank you.
speaker: JOSEPH MOORE - Analyst, Morgan Stanley

text: Sashia Hari, you may begin your conference.
speaker: JOSEPH MOORE - Analyst, Morgan Stanley

text: Thank you and good afternoon everyone.
speaker: JOSEPH MOORE - Analyst, Morgan Stanley

text: Welcome to NVIDIA's conference call for the first quarter of fiscal 2027.
speaker: TIMOTHY ARCURI - Analyst, UBS

text: With me today from NVIDIA, our Jensen Wong, president and chief executive officer,
speaker: STACY RASGON - Analyst, Bernstein Research

text: and Collette Kress, executive vice president and chief financial officer.
speaker: JOSHUA BUCHALTER - Analyst, TD Cowen

text: Our call is being webcast live on NVIDIA's investor relations website.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The webcast will be available for replay until the conference call to discuss
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our financial results for the second quarter of fiscal 2027.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The content of today's call is NVIDIA's property.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: It can't be reproduced or transcribed without our prior written consent.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: During this call, we may make forward-looking statements based on current expectations.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: These are subject to a number of significant risks and uncertainties,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and our actual results may differ materially.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: For a discussion of factors that could affect our future financial results and business,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: please refer to the disclosure in today's earnings release,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our most recent forms 10K and 10Q, and the reports that we may file on form 8K
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: All our statements are made as of today, May 20th, 2026, based on information currently available to us.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Except as required by law, we assume no obligation to update any such statements.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: During this call, we will discuss non-GAP financial measures.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: You can find a reconciliation of these non-GAP financial measures to GAP financial measures
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: in our CFO commentary, which is posted on our website.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: With that, let me turn the call over to Collette.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Thank you, Tashia. We delivered an exceptional quarter with revenue, operating income,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and free cash flow exceeding our prior records.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Total revenue of 82 billion was up 85% year over year and 20% sequentially.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: This marked our third consecutive quarter of year over year acceleration
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and the 14th straight quarter of sequential growth, a significant feat given the sheer size
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and complexity of our manufacturing operations.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The 13.5 billion sequential revenue increase was also a record.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We capitalized on the inflection in inference demand by ramping blackwell systems
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: across our diverse and customer base from hyperscalers to model makers to AI cloud providers
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and sovereign customers. In Q1, we also allocated capital effectively across our
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: indeed investments in our ecosystem and share repurchases.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We returned a record 20 billion to our shareholders while executing strategic investments,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: both upstream supply chain and downstream go-to-market ecosystem.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: This is critical to the market's development and our long-term position.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Data center revenue of 75 billion was up 92% year over year and 21% sequentially,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: driven by sustained strength in our blackwell architecture.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And demand for GB300 and VL72 was particularly strong with frontier model builders and hyperscalers,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: each having cumulatively deployed hundreds and thousands of blackwell GPUs, marking the fastest
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: product ramp in our company's history.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Race Blackwell is the fastest training system as well as the lowest token generation cost
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: at inference. Spectrum X, our end-to-end ethernet platform purpose built for AI,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: is now larger than all ethernet network peers combined. Infiniban has also had a very strong
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: quarter growing more than four X year over year driven by deployments of our next generation XDR
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: technology. Four year models, data center computing revenue of 60 billion was up 77% year over year
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: while data center networking revenue of 15 billion nearly tripled year over year.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Before we dive into data center, we'd like to brief you on our transition to a new reporting
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: framework that better reflects our current and future growth drivers. We have two market platforms,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: data center and edge computing. Within data center, we will report two sub-markets, hyperscale and
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: ACIE, which incorporates AI clouds, industrial and enterprise.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Hyperscale will include revenue from the public cloud and the world's largest consumer internet
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: companies, while ACIE addresses our growth opportunities in diverse AI purpose built data centers and
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: AI factories across industries and countries. Edge computing highlights devices for agentic
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and physical AI, including PCs, gaming consoles, workstations, AI-run based stations, robotics
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and automotive. For your reference, we have posted on our website a revenue breakdown
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: based on our new platforms for the past nine quarters. Moving back to our data center results,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: hyperscale revenue of 38 billion was approximately 50% of data center revenue
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and increased 12% quarter over quarter. ACIE revenue was 37 billion and grew 31%
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: quarter over quarter, including AI cloud revenue that more than tripled year over year.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Our customers have enabled rapid stand up of AI compute capacity. The number of partner data
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: centers exceeding 10 megawatts has nearly doubled in just one year. Now surpassing 80 sites,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: sovereign revenue increased more than 80% year over year. NVIDIA AI infrastructure is now deployed
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: across nearly 40 countries representing 50 trillion in GDP. As evident to our Q1 results,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our customer base is diverse and growing. Supported by our vast ecosystem and installed base,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: breadth of CUDA accelerated application and the lowest token cost provider. We are well positioned
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to address a market opportunity that far exceeds that of any other AI computing platform.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Demand for AI infrastructure continues to expand at an unprecedented pace. The build out of AI
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: factories is accelerating. The value of NVIDIA AI infrastructure is rising. The price of renting
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: an H100 has risen 20% year to date, while a 100 cloud pricing is up nearly 15%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Benefiting from the versatility of our platform and continuous performance
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: enhancements enhanced by our software stack, customers are generating profitable revenue
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: beyond the depreciable life of their GPUs. The vast and trusted marketplace for NVIDIA compute
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: is a critical foundation on which billions in AI infrastructure spending is being financed
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: by the ecosystem. There are two primary drivers behind the accelerating build out of AI infrastructure.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: First, from search and advertising to recommender systems and content understanding. The largest
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: hyperscale workloads continue to transition from CPU to GPU based accelerating computing.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Second, the adoption of products and services native to AI is inflecting. Since the advent of
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: chat GPT, we have witnessed mainstream AI transition from one-shot inference to reasoning and to now
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: agentic. AI is no longer a nice to have. AI is now a necessity for enhancing productivity across
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: all industries and roles. This is propelling revenue acceleration across all layers of the AI
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: cake, including energy, chips, infrastructure, models, and applications. Growth in the model layer,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: particularly at anthropic and open AI, has been incredible with momentum continuing to accelerate,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: including breakout growth in open AI's codecs since the launch of GPT 5.5.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: With analysts now forecasting hyperscale capex to exceed 1 trillion in 2027 and agentic AI beginning
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to proliferate all industries. AI infrastructure spending is on track to reach 3 to 4 trillion
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: annually by the end of this decade. Our Blackwell architecture is everywhere, adopted and deployed
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: by every major hyperscaler, every cloud provider, and every major model maker. Last month, we celebrated
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: opening AI's launch of GPT 5.5 co-designed for trained with and served on Blackwell,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: currently positioned at the top of artificial analysis leaderboards.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Microsoft's Fairwater, the world's most powerful AI data center, is now live, ahead of scheduled,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: powered by hundreds of thousands of Blackwell GPUs. Starting this year, AWS will add more than
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: 1 million Blackwell and Rubin GPUs and are collaborating on spectrum networking. At Google,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Blackwell will be offered to customers in the cloud, including confidential computing capability,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: a new foundation for secure high-performance AI. Our share of frontier AI compute is increasing.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We have deepened our collaboration with Anthropic and are delighted to be a strategic partner to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: expand their compute capacity. We will support the company's growth trajectory through AWS,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Azure, CoreWe, SpaceX AI, and more. Now with the addition of Anthropic 2, OpenAI, Gemini, SpaceX
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: AI, Meta, MSL, Microsoft AI, TML, reflection, perplexity, cursor, and other major frontier labs
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: already building on NVIDIA. Our share of frontier AI models will grow significantly.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Today's data centers are revenue generating AI factories, constrained by power and capital
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: AI factory operators must choose the right architecture. With our extreme co-design approach,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: we deliver the industry's lowest token cost, the highest token throughput, and the highest ROI.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: MLPerf inference results are in, and once again, we swept every benchmark, as Blackwell Ultra
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: delivered the highest throughput across the broad set of models and deployment scenarios.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Full stack innovations drove the 2.7x increase in throughput and a 60% reduction in the cost
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: per token on GB300 compared to just six months ago. NVIDIA compute is not just the highest
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: performance AI infrastructure, it is the most economic and financeable. Customers do not buy
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: GPUs, they build AI factories, and the right economic metric is not the purchase price of the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: GPU, it is the lifetime cost of an AI factory producing intelligence, token per watt, tokens per dollar,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: uptime, utilization, time to production, software durability, and asset life. NVIDIA excels at all
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: of them. Agentic AI and reinforcement learning represents new growth opportunities for CPUs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Building on the success of our base CPU, Vera is arriving just in time to meet this inflection.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Built on custom ARM cores and co-designed end-to-end with Ruby GPU and NVLink, Vera will deliver up to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: 1.5x faster performance per core, 2x performance per watt, and 4x density per rack compared to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: x86 based alternatives. Vera CPU opens a brand new 200 billion town for NVIDIA, a market we have
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: never addressed before, and every major hyperscale and system maker is partnering with us to get it
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: deployed. We have visibility to nearly 20 billion in total CPU revenue this year, setting us up to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: become the world leading CPU supplier. Our annual product casements, a pace that is unmatched,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: remains a key pillar supporting our market position. We are on track to commence production
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: shipments of Vera Rubin in the second half of this year, starting in Q3. By integrating
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: seven purpose-built chips across five accelerated racks, Vera Rubin will deliver up to 35x higher
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: inference throughput and up to 10x greater AI factory revenue compared with Blackwell.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: As an early adopter, Google's A5x bare metal instances which can support up to 960,000
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Rubin GPUs across multiple sites can enable customers to run their largest AI workloads
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: on NVIDIA's optimized infrastructure. While the US government has approved licenses for H200 to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: be shipped to China-based customers, we have yet to generate any revenue and we are uncertain
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: whether any imports will be allowed into the country. As a result, consistent with last quarter,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: we are not including any China data center compute revenue in our outlook.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Let me move to edge computing. Our edge computing market platform generated 6.4 billion, up 10%
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: quarter over quarter and 29% year over year. Robust Blackwell workstation demand was a strong
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: contributor to the growth while consumer demand fell modestly due to higher memory and system prices.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Our physical AI continues to gain momentum exceeding 9 billion in revenue over the last 12 months.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Our partnership with Uber will power the Robotaxy fleet across nearly 30 cities and four continents
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: by 2028 and in robotics leading companies across a range of industrial, surgical and humanoid
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: applications are building on NVIDIA's technology to develop and deploy at scale.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We remain front-footed in securing sufficient supply to support our customer's growth.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: In Q1, we increased total supply inclusive of inventory, purchase commitments on pre-page
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to 145 billion. While we are not immune to supply challenges,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: remain confident in our ability to support the growth opportunity ahead. With our intense focus,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: scale and long-standing partnerships with critical suppliers continuing to serve us well.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Let me move to the rest of the P&L. Gap gross margin was 74.9% and non-gap gross margins was 75%.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Largely flat sequentially by Blackwell systems continued to account for most of our shipments.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Gap and non-gap operating expenses were up 12% sequentially primarily due to higher compensation
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and an increase in compute and infrastructure costs. Our non-gap effective tax rate of 16%
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: came just below our prior outlook due to favorable geographic mix.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And on our balance sheet, days sales outstanding was 45 days due to favorable timing of collections.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We expect to return to the mid-50s in Q2. We generated record free cash flow 49 billion up from 35
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: billion in Q4. I'd now like to update you on our capital allocation plan. First, to reiterate,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our intention is to prioritize R&D and strategic investment. Both will enable us to cultivate
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our ecosystem, drive market growth, and strengthen our market position. As a key enabler of AI,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: we will make investments necessary to deliver the industry's lowest cost per token and the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: highest token throughput, which will help our customers and partners scale and expand the AI
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: frontier. Return program is another key component of our capital allocation strategy.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Given confidence in our long-term free cash flow outlook and our commitment to sharing our
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: successful shareholders, we are increasing our quarterly dividend from 1 cent to 20 cents per
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: share. We plan to review our dividend on a regular basis as we continue to scale our business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We are also announcing an 80 billion share repurchase authorization, which is in addition
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to the 39 billion remaining on our current plan. As we indicated at GTC, we plan to return roughly
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: 50 percent of free cash flow to shareholders this year. Let me turn to the outlook for the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: second quarter. Total revenue is expected to be 91 billion plus or minus 2 percent. We expect
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: sequential growth to be driven primarily by data center. We are continuing to work vigorously
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: on our supply chain ecosystem to address the incredible demand we see ahead of us, giving us
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: full confidence in the 1 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue we foresee from 2025 through
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: calendar 2027. Gap and non-gap gross margins are expected to be 74.9 and 75 percent, respectively,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: plus or minus 50 basis points. For the full year, we are still expecting to be in the mid-70s.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Gap and non-gap operating expenses are expected to be approximately 8.5 billion and 8.3 billion
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: respectively. For the full year, we now expect up X growth to grow somewhere in the upper 40s
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: on a year-over-year basis driven by higher R&D and acceleration in the usage of AI tools
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to enhance productivity. For the full year 2027, we expect gap and non-gap tax rates to be between
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: 16 and 18 percent, excluding any discrete items from material changes to our tax environment.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: This is lower than our prior expectation of 17 to 19 due to changes in geographic mix.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: That puts me at the end of this part, and I'm going to now turn this over to the Q&A with Toshiya.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Thanks, Collette. We will now transition to Q&A. Operator, please pull for questions.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Thank you. At this time, I would like to remind everyone in order to ask a question,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: press star, then the number one on your telephone keypad. We'll pause for just a moment to compile
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the Q&A roster. As a reminder, please limit yourself to one question. Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Your first question comes from Joseph Moore with Morgan Stanley. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Great. Thank you for letting me ask the question. I guess I'd like to ask,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: what drove the change in segmentation? What's the philosophy behind giving us the numbers that way?
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And then can you talk about any competitive differences between the two segments and this
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: surprising CPU number that you talked about? How do you see that across the two segments as well?
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Thank you. Yeah, thanks, Joe. First of all, Collette meant to say we're increasing our quarterly
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: dividends from one cent to 25 cents. I think that extra five cents would mean a lot to the large
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: shareholders. Anyhow, let's see, Joe, on the segmentation and the description of the business.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We wanted you to understand our business better. AI is very diverse and computing is diverse.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: They're diverse in several ways. The first thing, of course, is AI includes languages and
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: depending on the different industries, it could be 3D graphics or manufacturing and industrial
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: robotics. It could be proteins for life sciences. It could be small chemicals for life sciences
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: or material sciences. It could be physics for the physical sciences, whether it's in the energy
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: sector or, of course, the science labs, higher education, so on and so forth. So AI is diverse.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The second thing is the applications of diverse. It could be in enterprise. It could be in the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: energy sector, manufacturing sector and such. Where it runs is diverse. It could be in the,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: you know, hyperscale cloud. It could be AI natives. These are a whole network of AI natives that are
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: cropping up around the world. Enterprises on-prem, industrial in the factories, in the plants,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: all the way to super computing centers and the edge, edge including, of course,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: what most people see, self-driving cars, robotics, but a large growing network of
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: computers inside manufacturing plants, whether it's a chip plant or packaging or computer plants,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: all kinds of different types of manufacturing plants. And then, of course, in the future,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: every single base station, every single radio network would become an AI powered radio network.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And so where it runs. And then lastly, how it's governed? You know, it could be operated by public
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: cloud, but it could also have regular industrial regulatory reasons that prevents it from being
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: run in a regulatory cloud. It could be because of confidential computing. It could be because of
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: national security reasons. Different data centers have to be built differently.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: NVIDIA is quite unique in the sense that we are the only company that builds all of the technology
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: components. We build it in an extreme co-design way, in a complete end-to-end way, and a full stack way.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: But then we, of course, open the platform so that it could be integrated into all the different
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: environments. But some environments just require, and enterprise, for example, require a company
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: who has all of the technologies working together so that they don't have to build it. They would
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: like to buy it and operate it. And so there's many different segments of the data center market,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: where NVIDIA is a total solution, fully integrated solution with full stack, but still open, that
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: way of producing or delivering products is really, really important. And so if you look at our
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: different segments, the way we broke it out, into three large segments. You take all of the words
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: that I just said, and you try to find the simplest factoring of it, it would be the hyperscale clouds
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: that would be one large segment. And within that segment, there's three different ways that we
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: operate. First way is that we help the hyperscale clouds accelerate their data processing and machine
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: learning workloads. We accelerate and support their AI processing inside. We also, of course,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: bring a lot of business and video ecosystem business to their public clouds. And so that's
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: one segment. The second segment is AI natives, enterprise on-prem, industrial on-prem,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and sovereign AI. That segment is growing incredibly fast, because everybody needs AI.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And we're going to see AI being adopted by every industry, every country, every company.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And so everybody wants to build it in a different way. And the fact that we provide the entire
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: solution, it makes it much easier, makes it possible at all for people to be able to build
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: these things. And then, of course, the robotic edge. Today, yesterday's computing was largely
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: about personal computing. In the future, it's going to be about personal AI. And that personal
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: AI, one example of it is the self-driving car. It's a car. It's a robotic system that's
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: essentially your personal AI. And of course, there'll be all kinds of different types of
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: robotic systems, including even the base station radio network, as I mentioned,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: is going to be essentially a robotic system. So that's the reason why we broke it all apart
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: this way. It's the simplest way of understanding our business. Each one of them have different
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: stacks in a lot of ways. They have different operating systems. They operate in a different way.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We go to market very differently in each one of them. The easiest go to market, of course,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: is the hyperscaler, because there are only five or six of them. But the rest of them,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the rest of the industry, represents a couple of 250,000 companies around the world.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: That go to market is very complex, very diverse. Your understanding of AI has to be extremely
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: diverse. And as you know, NVIDIA has a large, the largest suite of acceleration libraries in the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: world, from computational lithography to fluid dynamics to particle physics to molecular dynamics
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to the list goes on. And all of those libraries are essential for us to engage the vertical industries
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: that represents the second and the third category. Okay, so anyways, it's really about the fact that
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: our business has now evolved and grown to such a large scale. It's helpful to segment it so that
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: you have a better understanding of how our business works. Your next question comes from Ben
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: writes us with Amelia's research. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Hey guys, thanks so much. I wanted to ask Jensen, I want to ask you about your philosophy on growth.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: end of the decade. I was just wondering, you know, the goal for the company to grow faster than

text: hyperscaler cap X. Do you still, are you comfortable in kind of endorsing that view? And, you know,

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: cap X. And the reason for that is illustrated by the segmentation that I just described.

text: the first part is hyperscalers. That's the that's the hyperscale cap X that that that you were just
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: talking about. And there are trillion dollars this year. I have every expectation it's going to grow
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: from here for fundamentally good reasons. This is the way computing is going to work in the future.

text: And if they don't have to compute, they won't have the revenues.

text: It is very clear compute is revenues compute is profit. And so
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the world is changing software didn't use to use SaaS didn't use to use as much compute,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: but AI requires a tremendous amount of compute. But you could do, of course, incredibly more,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: which is the reason why we heard about the AI frontier AI companies,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: both anthropic and open AI growing at an incredible pace. The fact that they can grow within one month.

text: What SaaS what some of the SaaS companies would have taken a decade to grow

text: is all of the AI native clouds, they're regional, they're all over the place. Their startups all

text: over the world supporting those companies. Their enterprise, 250,000 enterprise companies around
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the world, many of them will have to build or want to build AI factories for themselves to operate.

text: Many industrial companies, there's no choice but to put the computer where the context is,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: where the action is. You can't put that in the cloud. It has to respond reliably,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: quickly, every single time. Can't imagine, you know, a chip plant, a chip that being connected
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to a cloud service provider doesn't make any sense. And so the second category and the software
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: in AI clouds. And so there's a whole, there's a whole category of data centers that
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: semi custom chips just don't apply because these data centers want to buy systems,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: they want to operate systems, they don't want to design, they don't want to build it themselves.

text: And so the second category is extremely diverse. Instead of

text: the second category is hundreds, thousands of companies. And in the future, it would be

text: This set, second category, when I talk about physical AI and I talk about how the rest of the

text: you could see that in fact we're growing share in the hyperscalers because we now have

text: very few companies have exposure into the second category because of the platform solution that we
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: have. Your next question comes from CJ Muse with Cantor Fitzgerald. Your line is open.

text: And we're growing share and inference very, very quickly. And the reason for that is this year,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the number of frontier model companies grew. And so there's cursor and perplexity and

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: frontier model company will jump on Vera Rubin from the get-go. And that wasn't true before on
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: just explained in the inference question is really focused on hyperscale. Remember, there's a whole
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: second category of AI data centers that we serve almost uniquely. This segment is very fragmented,

text: requires a fairly integrated, really well integrated platform solution and a very large go-to market.

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Your next question comes from Timothy Arcuring with UBS. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Thanks a lot. I'm Jensen. I wanted to ask about the traction you're getting with some of these

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: that and maybe, you know, also how that fits into your broader platform strategy? Thanks.

text: The LPX is designed for low latency and high token rate. But it's throughput is low. It's
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: throughput is low. It's model size capacity is low. And it's context processing. It's ability to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: absorb a lot of context, for example, for software coding. For agent workloads.

text: It's ability to absorb a great deal of context is lower. And so the challenge is simply,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: and I've explained before that the use case for LPX is not broad. It's, you know, intended for
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: somebody who has a fairly large portfolio of different types of token services. And for the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: high token rate, maybe these services are quite, quite premium. And the number of customers is

text: not significant, but the token rate is very high. And so that remains exactly consistent with what
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: I've said before. And I still expect that. And so so I, I, I expect that LPX and other, other S RAM based
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: decode, decode focus, token, you know, high token rate generate generated focus accelerators
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: will always be, will be a niche product for some time, for some time to come. You know, as you know,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: preparing for training, okay, data processing to pre training to post training reinforcement
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: learning, all the way to inference. Grace Blackwell is the best platform in the world to do all of that.

text: And if we, if insert certain certain circumstances, so long as the customer, the provider already has a

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: that service even better. And so that's how I see the market. And I think, I think whether it's
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: 20% or 10%, just depends on, on, on where we are in the development of AI. I think today is a lot
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: less than 20%. Someday, these premium tokens could be 20%. And, and I'm, you know, we're ready to
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: work with work with, with service providers to enable this capability. I'm excited about it.

text: Your next question comes from Vivek Aria with Bank of America Securities. Your line is open.

text: Thanks for taking my question. Um, Jensen, there's a lot of excitement around CPU for

text: agentic applications and just a lot of noise around the number of CPUs, actually exceeding the

text: number of, of GPUs. And I was just hoping that you could kind of, you know, give your perspective

text: that first of all, you know, is this an incremental workload? Is this kind of cannibalizing what the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: GPU would have done otherwise? And then secondly, the 20 billion number that you gave, is that for

text: standalone, where are CPUs or is that kind of already included in that Vera as part of Vera
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Ruben? So just, if you could educate us, educate us on, you know, the role of CPU versus GPU,

text: is it cannibalistic? Is it incremental? And then the 20 billion number, how to kind of put that in

text: context with what you sell, right? Which is usually the CPU as part of the GPU. Thank you.

text: The 20 billion is for standalone CPU. And, and remember, we have, we have Vera is used in three
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: ways as a standalone, four ways as a, let me start with the one that you already know,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: very, the first way is Vera Ruben. And we'll sell millions of Rubens. And every two of them is
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: connected to a Vera. And of course, we price those two. And, and, and they're properly priced. And so,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: so that's number one use case. The second use case is Vera standalone CPU. The third is Vera
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: with CX nine. And it's in the software stack for storage. And then Vera in a, with CX nine,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: with a software stack for security and compute isolation and confidential computing. Okay, so each
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: one of those use cases is built on Vera. And my sense is that will be supply constrained
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: throughout, throughout the entire life of Vera Ruben. There are four different use cases of it.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And, but anyhow, anyhow, the, the answer to your question is of the 20 billion is a standalone.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: With respect to CPU use, an agent, an agent is essential, what people call a harness.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The agent has, has a, has a harness that does the, and the harness could be open claw, it could be
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Hermes code, cloud code, cloud code is essentially a harness around cloud around the opus model.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Open AI's codecs is a harness around the GPT 5.5 model. And so these are harnesses and these
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: harnesses provide for things like IO, orchestration, memory management, tool use connected to tools,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: for example, browsers and things like that, C compilers, Python compilers. And so,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the harness runs on CPU and the tool use runs on CPUs. You know, so for example, if the, if the
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: AI were to do a search or do a, do a, a browser, use a browser, that would run on the CPU.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The world has, the world has a billion users, human users. My sense is that the world is going
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to have billions of agents, not today. I mean, we're going to grow into it, but we'll have
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: billions of agents and those billions of agents will all use tools. And those tools that can be
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: like, you know, like PCs, just like us humans using, using PCs today, in the future, you'll have
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: an agent using PC. And so if you kind of think along the lines of, in the future, you pick your
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: favorite number of agents at the moment, you know, at the moment, call it a few hundred thousand,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: but in the future call it, you know, eventually a few billion. I could imagine them all using,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: using the effective effectively having PCs that they can all use. And so, so, but the large line,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: every one of those, every one of those agents are going to spin off subagents. And every time they
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: spin these off, you're going to need to do inference. That's where the thinking happens.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: All of the thinking happens on GPUs. All of the orchestration essentially runs on CPUs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And, and, and the subagents, when they're spun off, they, when they're thinking they use GPUs.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Whenever, whenever the agents use simulators, those can run on CPUs or GPUs, which is the reason
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: we're working so closely with cadence and synopsis and to accelerate all of the world's tools. We're
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: accelerating all of the world's tools and data processing engines and database,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: database engines, because agents use these tools and have, you know, they have lower patients tolerance
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: them humans and they want things to happen quickly. And so, we're accelerating all the world's tools
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: so that it runs on CUDA. And you could see us doing that, you know, when I work with, when I work with
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: cadence and synopsis and Siemens and, you know, companies in Adobe, and that's because we're
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: trying to get all of the world's tools to run on, on GPUs, because they already have GPUs and it's
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: a lot faster. So, so we're going to need a lot more CPUs. And Vera was designed to be an agentic
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: CPU. The CPUs of the past were designed to have many cores so that it could be easily rentable.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: People rent a course. Well, agents don't rent cores. They just want the work to be done fast.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The economics of the past was dollars per core. That's the economics of cloud computing of the past.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The economics of the future is tokens per dollar or dollars per token. And so, what we need to do in
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the future is to generate tokens, process tokens as fast as possible. And that's what Vera does
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: incredibly well. So, we're expecting to be very successful with Vera. But, you know,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: ultimately, ultimately, what we're doing is we're building infrastructure for AI. And it needs
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: incredibly great storage. That's the reason why we built STX. It needs incredibly good networking.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: That's why we have spectrum X. It needs incredibly great GPUs, of course, and inferencing ability.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: That's the reason why MVLink 72. It needs incredibly great security and confidential
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: computing, which is the reason why Vera Rubens the world's first platform with end-to-end confidential
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: computing. And it needs, you know, great CPUs. We've got it all covered.

text: Your next question comes from Stacy Raskin with Bernstein Research. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Hi, guys. Thanks for taking my question. I wanted to go back to the segmentation. So, first of all,

text: I'm just curious, where do you put the neoclouds across those two segments? Are they in hyperscale

text: or are they in the AI cloud? Part of me assumes the latter, but I'm not so sure. And then,

text: but just a magnitude of them, I mean, they're both about the same magnitude now.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: It almost sounded to me like you were suggesting that you thought the latter, the AI cloud would
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: grow faster, maybe going forward than hyperscale. Is that what you're trying to say? Or do you do

text: see like the same kind of growth coming from both segments? First of all, you're correct that AI
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: native clouds, AI native clouds don't build chips, don't design their own chips, and they don't want
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to, they can't really assemble, you know, could unrelated parts together into an AI factory.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And their time, their, their patience, their tolerance for time to first token is extremely low.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And their, their need for an architecture that has a great deal of offtake so that it runs every model has customers from everywhere is incredibly high.

text: And so that's the reason why NVIDIA's architecture is so perfect for them.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: We offer every component and whatever we don't offer, our ecosystem of partners offer it, and it's all fully integrated, it all works together.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The number of customers that could rent it from an AI native is incredibly high. Basically, every single AI builder, every, you know, every AI native startup around the world,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: SaaS companies, enterprise companies, industrial companies. And so, so our, our computing, our architecture is the most
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: rentable of any computing platform in the world. So it's the, it's the most performant. It's the easiest to put together.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: It's the most rentable, has the best TCO, and it's the easiest to finance.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And so all of those properties are quite unique to the needs of AI natives. It's in the second category. They're very similar to,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to even OEMs and so on and so forth, large enterprises and so forth, surprisingly.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Okay, so we put that in the second category. If you look at, look at that segment, it started growing after
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: the AI ecosystem developed in the hyperscale. Hyperscale developed the AI first for a lot of reasons. You know, they have great computer science.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: They have excellent data center capability and they also
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: focus largely on consumer applications, which if not perfect, is not the end of the world. It enhances the service so long as it enhances the service.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And so for many of the other applications, industrial applications, enterprise applications, until the AI is very capable and does really productive work and does it safely and
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: it could do it in a way that can actually generate impact and income. It doesn't really get used. And so you expect the second category
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: to develop slower than hyperscale and you could see that in the numbers. However, long term,
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: if you look at industrial and enterprise, clearly that's where future economics is going to be because it represents some, you know, 50, 80 trillion dollars of the world's economy.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And so, and it's going to be larger than that because of AI. And so I, I expect the second category to be to be larger over time.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Both in the near term over the next several years. I think it's a short, it's a, it's a foregone conclusion. Both are going to grow incredibly fast.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: I expect the second category to still grow faster, but both are going to grow incredibly fast.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And then I'm hoping that within the next five years, a physical AI and robotic segment is going to grow incredibly fast.

text: Your next question comes from Jim Schneider with Bolson Saks. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Good afternoon. Thanks for taking my question.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Back at GTC, I believe you discussed a trillion dollar visibility into both your, or Ruben and Blackwell platform revenue, but I believe that excluded things like LPX, Ruben CPX and the Vera CPU racks.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Could you maybe give us a sense about whether the Vera CPUs are going to be the biggest source of upside above and beyond that one trillion dollars? Are you contemplating other sort of combinations of products, including CPUs

text: that would allow you to gain an even greater share of that total TAM? Thank you.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: In terms of incremental above the trillion, I would say one, the continued growing of share of the frontier AI models.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: I'm expecting to grow more share. And so I'm expecting that to grow.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Number two, we didn't include any Vera CPU, standalone CPU in that number. And so I expect that to be the second largest.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The TAM is of course quite large in agents, agent systems, and all of our customers are quite excited about Vera.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And we're going to sell a whole bunch of Vera's.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And then third would be LPX, because as I explained earlier, LPX is designed as a, because of its SRAM architecture has the benefit of very low latency and very, very high interactivity.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: But it's also its throughput and its context processing ability is also quite limited. And that's just kind of the nature of SRAM type based systems.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And, but the combination, you know, we'll be able to address, address the entire spectrum of AI from from pre training to post training to inference, agent systems through the combination of Vera and Vera Rubin and LPN.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Your next question comes from Joshua Buckhalter with TD Cowan. Your line is open.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Hey guys, thanks for taking my question and congrats on the great results.

text: Collab, leaving your prepared remarks, you mentioned GB 300 is sort of the fastest ramp in the company's history.

text: Similar rack. Does that mean we should expect a similar slope to the Vera Rubin ramp as a GB 300 or should it be a bit more gradual given the new Silicon? Thank you.

speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And then once we get to Q four, we're probably going to start to see our ramping continue. It's hard to say at this point what will be a faster ramp.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: But again, we have demand already planned. We've got POs. We've got almost all of our major customers ready to go.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: And these are very complex systems that we need to put together. So I think it's just about the timing that it's going to take for us to get that into market.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: Nothing else other than getting from production of all of the different systems that we have ready for ordered. So little early to say, but yes, we're going to start in Q three and continue to ramp into Q four and Q one of next year certainly certainly is going to be very big as well.

text: There are no further questions at this time. Tashiahari, I turn the call back over to you.

text: Thank you. Before I hand it over to Jensen, please note, Jensen will be giving the keynote at GTC Taipei at Computex on June 1st. We will also be participating at the TD Cowan TMT conference on May 28th and the Bank of America Global Technology Conference on June 4th.

text: Our earnings call to discuss the results of our second quarter of fiscal 2027 is scheduled for August 26th. With that, here's Jensen to close us up.

text: This was an extraordinary quarter. The man who has gone parabolic. The reason is simple. Agentic AI has arrived. AI can now do productive and valuable work. Tokens are now profitable. So model makers are in a race to produce more.

text: In the AI era, compute capacity is revenue and profits. NVIDIA is the platform of this era of all the platforms in the world. NVIDIA Compute Supports the richest diversity of demand.

text: Let me highlight my top five things. First, NVIDIA is the only platform that runs every frontier AI model. With the addition of anthropic to our existing partners, Open AI is the only platform that runs every frontier AI model.

text: We are in every hyperscale cloud supporting their core data processing and machine learning workloads, internal AI services, as well as supporting their demand for NVIDIA users in their public cloud services.

text: Our full stack complete AI factory solution and vast global ecosystem let us uniquely address new AI data center segments, new AI cloud natives, new AI native clouds, and sovereign AI clouds, and on premises enterprise and industrial infrastructure.

text: This is that second category I was talking about earlier.

text: Fourth, NVIDIA CUDA extends all the way to the edge. Robotics, autonomous vehicles, embedded medical instruments, AI ran telco base stations. The next wave is physical AI with billions of autonomous and robotic systems operating in the physical world.

text: This is the third segment we were talking about earlier. And rounding out the top five things, we have a major new growth driver, Vera, the world's first CPU purpose built for agent AI.

text: Vera opens a brand new $200 billion TAN for NVIDIA, a market we have never addressed before. And every major hyperscaler and system maker is partnering with us to deploy it.
speaker: OPERATOR - Sarah

text: The world is rebuilding computing for agent AI and robotic physical AI. NVIDIA sits at the center of these transitions. We built NVIDIA compute platform over three decades.

text: One architecture, vast ecosystem, extreme co-designed across chips, systems, networking and software. We built it ahead of this moment so that when agent AI arrived and NVIDIA would be ready.
